content.text: Cost and efficiency determine whether an agent that works in a demo can be afforded in production. Agents are token-hungry: they loop, call tools, carry long contexts, and often run many trajectories per task, so a naive design can cost orders of magnitude more than a single model call for the same outcome.

Cost efficiency for agents is the practice of achieving a target quality at the lowest total cost of ownership — API spend, latency, and compute — across a full multi-step workload. It treats tokens and calls as a budget to be allocated deliberately, spending on the steps that need capability and economising everywhere else.

The problem is structurally different from single-call optimisation because agent cost is a product of several multiplying factors: turns per task, tokens per turn (which grow as context accumulates), and trajectories per task when sampling or search is used. Small per-factor savings compound, and small per-factor bloat compounds just as fast.

Unit economics decide which agent products are viable. A support agent that costs more per resolution than a human agent cannot be deployed at scale; a research agent whose every query costs dollars limits its own adoption. Efficiency is not a nicety — it is frequently the gate between a promising prototype and a shippable product.

It also matters for latency and throughput. A long chain of large-context calls is slow, and slow agents lose users. Under load, inefficient token use throttles how many concurrent sessions a fixed budget can serve. Cost, latency, and scale are the same problem viewed from three angles.
— how input and output tokens are billed, and why output is usually costlier.
— reusing computed prefixes to avoid re-billing static context.
— the capability/cost spectrum from small to frontier models.
— the trade-offs between batching, streaming, and parallelism.

Total agent cost per task is approximately the number of turns times the average tokens per turn times the per-token price, summed over trajectories. Because context accumulates, tokens per turn tend to grow across a task, making later turns disproportionately expensive.

Not every step needs a frontier model. Routing sends easy sub-tasks to a cheap, fast model and reserves the expensive model for the hard ones. A cascade tries the cheap model first and escalates only when its answer fails a confidence check, capturing most of the savings while preserving quality.

Agents re-send large, static prefixes — system prompts, tool schemas, retrieved documents — on every turn. Caching the computed prefix means those tokens are billed at a steep discount on subsequent turns, which is one of the highest-leverage optimisations for looped agents.

Left unmanaged, context grows every turn and cost grows with it. Summarising or pruning history keeps the working context small, trading a little information for large, compounding savings on every remaining turn.

attrs.alt: Cost-aware routing: classify, route, cache, verify, escalate

content.text: The architecture concentrates spend where it buys quality and strips it everywhere else. A difficulty classifier and a confidence-gated cascade let the system serve the bulk of traffic on cheap models while still meeting quality on the hard tail.
A cascade's expected cost is a weighted sum: every task pays for the cheap model, and only the fraction
that escalates additionally pays for the expensive one. If the cheap model handles a fraction
is small — most tasks are easy — the cascade approaches the cheap model's cost while retaining the expensive model's quality on the hard minority. The escalation rate
is the key lever, controlled by the confidence threshold.
A cost-aware cascade tries a cheap model, verifies, and escalates only on low confidence. Caching the static prefix cuts the recurring token cost of the agent loop.

import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

CHEAP = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
STRONG = "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929"

def cascade(prompt, verify, system, tools=None):
    """Try the cheap model; escalate to the strong model only if verification fails."""
    cheap = client.messages.create(
        model=CHEAP, max_tokens=1024, system=system,
        tools=tools or [], messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}])
    answer = "".join(b.text for b in cheap.content if hasattr(b, "text"))
    ok, _ = verify(answer)
    if ok:
        return {"answer": answer, "model": CHEAP}
    strong = client.messages.create(
        model=STRONG, max_tokens=1024, system=system,
        tools=tools or [], messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}])
    answer = "".join(b.text for b in strong.content if hasattr(b, "text"))
    return {"answer": answer, "model": STRONG}

# Prompt caching: mark the large static prefix so it is billed at a discount
# on every subsequent turn of the agent loop.
def cached_system_blocks(system_text, tool_schema_text):
    return [
        {"type": "text", "text": system_text},
        {"type": "text", "text": tool_schema_text,
         "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}},   # cache the heavy, static part
    ]

def trim_context(messages, keep_recent=6, summariser=None):
    """Bound context growth: summarise old turns, keep the recent window verbatim."""
    if len(messages) <= keep_recent or summariser is None:
        return messages
    old, recent = messages[:-keep_recent], messages[-keep_recent:]
    summary = summariser(old)                       # one compact summary turn
    return [{"role": "user", "content": f"Summary of earlier turns: {summary}"}] + recent

Think of an agent's budget like a household's electricity bill. The total is not one big appliance but many small draws running many hours: lights left on (accumulating context), a space heater used when a sweater would do (frontier model for easy tasks), and paying full rate at peak when off-peak caching was available. Nobody saving on power buys a smaller house; they turn off what is not needed and right-size each appliance to its job. Agent cost control is the same discipline applied to tokens and calls.
a workload runs at volume — savings multiply across every request.
a measurable fraction of tasks are easy and a confidence check is available.
the agent re-sends large static prefixes across many turns.
tasks are long and later turns carry history they no longer need.
volume is low — engineering effort can exceed the spend it saves.

High-volume support and document-processing agents live or die on unit cost. A realistic deployment routes the majority of routine tickets to a small model, caches the system prompt and knowledge base across the conversation, and escalates only ambiguous cases to a frontier model, cutting cost per resolution by a large factor while holding quality steady. The measured escalation rate becomes a first-class operational metric, watched as closely as accuracy.

The three levers — routing, caching, and context management — compound multiplicatively, so combined they often reduce cost by an order of magnitude relative to a naive frontier-only loop. Latency improves alongside cost, since cheap models are faster and cached prefixes skip recomputation. The main risk is quality regression from over-aggressive routing or trimming, which is why every optimisation must be paired with a quality gate measured on a held-out set.
— native provider features that discount repeated static context.
— libraries and gateways that direct requests across model tiers.
— dashboards that attribute spend to tasks, tools, and turns.
— discounted asynchronous processing for latency-tolerant workloads.
Cost controls interact with reliability and must not be applied blindly.
— gate every cost optimisation on a measured quality threshold so savings never silently degrade output.
— hard caps on tokens and spend per task prevent a runaway loop from causing a cost incident.
— ensure cached context cannot leak between users or serve stale data.
— under budget pressure, degrade predictably (shorter answers) rather than failing outright.

attrs.alt: The multiplying factors that determine total agent cost per task

content.text: Cost is a product, not a sum, so the biggest wins come from attacking the largest factor. For looped agents the static prefix re-sent every turn is often dominant, which is why caching frequently beats model choice; for sampling-heavy tasks the trajectory count dominates. Profiling reveals which factor to cut first.
A difficulty classifier decides how much to spend before work begins, allocating cheap models and small budgets to easy tasks and reserving frontier models for the hard tail.
Cost optimisation is often the difference between a viable product and an unshippable one, but aggressive optimisation can quietly erode quality if not gated.
— routing and caching can cut cost per task by an order of magnitude.
— cheap models and cached prefixes are faster, improving user experience.
— a fixed budget serves far more concurrent sessions when tokens are used efficiently.
— over-aggressive routing or trimming can degrade output if not gated on quality.
— cascades, classifiers, and cache management add moving parts.
— thresholds like the escalation rate must be tuned and re-tuned as traffic shifts.
— paying top price for easy tasks that a small model handles is the most common waste.
— re-sending un-cached system prompts and tool schemas every turn silently dominates cost.
— letting history accumulate makes every later turn more expensive.
— escalating on no signal either over-escalates (no savings) or under-escalates (quality loss).
— a runaway loop without a hard token/spend limit turns a bug into a cost incident.
Profile the cost factors first and attack the largest — often the cached prefix or trajectory count.
Route easy tasks to cheap models with a confidence gate; reserve frontier models for the hard tail.
Cache large static prefixes and trim or summarise old context on long tasks.
Gate every optimisation on a held-out quality metric so savings never silently degrade output.
Enforce hard per-task token and spend budgets to bound worst-case cost.
agent cost is just the price of one model call.
It is a product of turns, growing tokens per turn, and trajectories; naive designs cost orders of magnitude more than a single call.
using the most capable model everywhere is safest.
Most sub-tasks are easy; routing them to cheap models with a confidence gate preserves quality at a fraction of the cost.
Caching and context management cut cost with little quality risk, and routing preserves quality when paired with a verification gate.
Agent cost is multiplicative — turns times growing tokens-per-turn times trajectories — so inefficiency compounds fast.
Model routing and cascades serve easy tasks cheaply and escalate only the hard minority, controlled by the escalation rate.
Prompt caching discounts the large static prefixes that looped agents re-send every turn — a top-leverage saving.
Context management bounds token growth, cutting cost on every remaining turn of a long task.
Pair every cost optimisation with a quality gate; savings must never silently degrade output.
